title: INTERIOR DESIGN: different strokes for different folks
subtitle: Choose the word that best fits each gap (multiple choice).
passage:
Interior design for the average person has come on leaps and bounds since the advent of home makeover TV shows back in the 1970s, and one thing that these shows have made crystal clear is the distinctive nature of taste. After all, as the saying goes, beauty is in the eye of the beholder and this is definitely the case when it comes to how we decorate our homes.

Some people believe interior design should have a [[1]] look and feel, considering embellishments, such as cushions to be unnecessary as they detract from purpose and function. These people tend to splash out on a few timeless pieces of furniture from [[2]] designers or shops, rather than have lots of bits and pieces at a lower price point. They would choose [[3]] elegance over anything that could be considered [[4]] or showy. This [[5]] approach is synonymous with contemporary design. Replete with sleek fixtures and fittings, many [[6]] modern offices have adopted this look in order to denote a level of success, sophistication and quality.

Not everyone is into this however, and for the more [[7]] amongst us, when it comes to curtains, cushions, rugs and ornaments, the more [[8]] the approach, the better. These people enjoy being surrounded by items that will stand out and take centre stage. They are likely to choose [[9]], eye-catching colours for the walls to create a bold contrast with those of the furnishings.

Then, there are those who love an eclectic, vintage look. These people are often to be found at flea markets looking for items to collect that reflect the motifs and styles of bygone eras, from wallpaper and colour schemes to furniture and accessories. Take the 1980s, for instance, when bright, [[10]] colours were all the rage. While this style may still be loved by fans of vintage, others consider it to be [[11]] and a sign of poor taste.

At the end of the day, whatever your style preference, be it the wild and the wacky or something altogether more muted and [[12]], the best advice is probably to embrace it and enjoy it.

items.explainCorrect: Utilitarian = focused on practical use and function; cushions are rejected because they get in the way of purpose.

explainIfChosen.0: Garish means offensively bright — the opposite of stripping the room back to function.
explainIfChosen.2: Whimsical is playful and decorative; these people dislike extra decoration.
explainIfChosen.3: Minimalism is a look; here the stress is usefulness and function (utilitarian), not only empty space.

items.explainCorrect: Renowned designers or shops = respected, high-end sources for expensive timeless pieces.

explainIfChosen.1: Vulgar suggests bad taste — not the kind of label for prestige shopping.
explainIfChosen.2: Unassuming means modest and low-key; splashing out on statement pieces does not fit.
explainIfChosen.3: Garish designers is not a natural collocation for quality investment furniture.

items.explainCorrect: Understated elegance = quiet, refined style contrasted with showy flash.

explainIfChosen.0: Imposing elegance is an odd pairing; the contrast is subtle vs showy.
explainIfChosen.2: Vulgar does not collocate with the positive choice of elegance here.
explainIfChosen.3: Garish is the opposite of the restrained taste described.

items.explainCorrect: Ostentatious = designed to impress or show off wealth — parallel to showy.

explainIfChosen.0: Minimalistic is the opposite of flashy display.
explainIfChosen.2: Utilitarian stresses usefulness, not conspicuous display.
explainIfChosen.3: Whimsical is quirky, not necessarily loud or boastful.

items.explainCorrect: Minimalistic approach matches sleek contemporary lines and few ornaments.

explainIfChosen.0: Flamboyant is maximal and bold — the opposite of this paragraph.
explainIfChosen.2: Renowned describes fame, not the visual style of the approach.
explainIfChosen.3: Utilitarian was already the mindset; here the named design style is minimalistic.

items.explainCorrect: Swanky = stylishly luxurious and expensive — fits offices signalling success.

explainIfChosen.0: Garish is negative — unlikely for a deliberate corporate image.
explainIfChosen.1: Imposing offices is possible, but swanky better captures upscale polish.
explainIfChosen.3: Unassuming is low-key — opposite of showing status.

items.explainCorrect: Flamboyant people love drama, layers, and pieces that grab attention.

explainIfChosen.1: Imposing describes forceful presence; flamboyant better matches decorative excess.
explainIfChosen.2: Utilitarian types prefer the stripped-back look in the previous paragraph.
explainIfChosen.3: Understated is the opposite of maximal cushions and ornaments.

items.explainCorrect: Whimsical = playful and imaginative — the more of that energy, the better for this group.

explainIfChosen.0: Unassuming is too quiet for “the more the better” with standout décor.
explainIfChosen.1: Utilitarian rejects the decorative pile they enjoy.
explainIfChosen.3: Garish is harshly negative; whimsical keeps a positive playful tone.

items.explainCorrect: Imposing colours = strong and striking on the walls, matching bold contrast.

explainIfChosen.1: Minimalistic walls would not be described as eye-catching in this sense.
explainIfChosen.2: Unassuming colours are the opposite of wall colours that shout for attention.
explainIfChosen.3: Understated is the opposite of the bold clash they want.

items.explainCorrect: Garish = unpleasantly bright — fits loud 1980s palettes critics dislike.

explainIfChosen.1: Minimalistic is the opposite of loud decade-defining brights.
explainIfChosen.2: Swanky suggests upscale polish, not necessarily neon excess.
explainIfChosen.3: Unassuming is too mild for the era described.

items.explainCorrect: Vulgar = in bad taste — parallel to a sign of poor taste.

explainIfChosen.0: Renowned means famous — you would not call the style “renowned” as a criticism.
explainIfChosen.2: Understated is usually positive or neutral, not “poor taste”.
explainIfChosen.3: Utilitarian is about function, not moral judgement of taste.

items.explainCorrect: Unassuming = modest and restrained — pairs with muted as the quiet alternative to wild.

explainIfChosen.0: Garish is loud — opposite of muted in this contrast.
explainIfChosen.1: Renowned does not describe a low-key personal style here.
explainIfChosen.3: Whimsical belongs to the playful maximal side, not the quiet end.
